news_summary: Recent breakthroughs in physics, computing, neuroscience, and ancient mathematics are reshaping our understanding of quantum mechanics, advancing future computer technology, revealing new insights into brain function, and decoding historical scientific knowledge.
news_summary_fr: Les avancées récentes en physique, en informatique, en neurosciences et en mathématiques anciennes redéfinissent notre compréhension de la mécanique quantique, font progresser les technologies informatiques de demain, apportent un éclairage nouveau sur le fonctionnement du cerveau et permettent de décrypter les connaissances scientifiques historiques.
news_summary_es: Los recientes avances en física, informática, neurociencia y matemáticas antiguas están redefiniendo nuestra comprensión de la mecánica cuántica, impulsando el desarrollo de la tecnología informática del futuro, aportando nuevos conocimientos sobre el funcionamiento del cerebro y descifrando el saber científico histórico.

articles.title: Physicists say quantum mechanics may not need imaginary numbers after all
articles.summary: Physicists from Heinrich Heine University Düsseldorf (HHU) have examined a fundamental property of quantum mechanics in collaboration with the German Aerospace Center (DLR). In the scientific journal Physical Review Letters, they show that this theory does not necessarily need to be formulated with imaginary numbers – real numbers can in fact also be used. The American Physical Society has also dedicated a “Highlight” to these findings in its Physics Magazine.
articles.content:
Quantum mechanics is the branch of physics that explains how matter and energy behave at the atomic and sub atomic scale. Developed in the early 1900s by pioneers including Max Planck, Niels Bohr, Werner Heisenberg, and Erwin Schrödinger, it has become one of the most successful scientific theories ever created.

The theory accurately describes a wide range of microscopic phenomena. These include the famous double slit experiment, in which particles also display wave like behavior, and quantum tunneling, where particles have a probability of passing through a barrier even when they do not have enough energy to overcome it in the classical sense. Other key quantum effects, such as entanglement and coherence, now form the foundation of emerging technologies including quantum computing and quantum communication.

Are Complex Numbers Really Essential?

For decades, quantum mechanics has relied on complex numbers, which combine a real component with an imaginary component. In the mathematical description of a quantum state, the real part represents the amplitude, while the imaginary part represents the phase. This framework has long been considered essential for describing many quantum processes.

Even so, physicists have continued to debate whether complex numbers are truly a fundamental part of nature or simply a convenient mathematical tool. That question naturally leads to another: Could quantum mechanics be formulated using only real numbers?

Revisiting a Key Quantum Assumption

A 2021 study concluded that complex numbers are indispensable under the standard postulates of quantum mechanics (Renou et al., Nature 600, 625 (2021)). Experimental results also supported that conclusion.

Researchers from Heinrich Heine University Düsseldorf (HHU) and the German Aerospace Center (DLR), led by Professor Dr Dagmar Bruß and doctoral researcher Pedro Barrios Hita, decided to take another look at the assumptions behind that earlier work.

In a new study published in Physical Review Letters, they found that one of the postulates used in the 2021 analysis was more restrictive than necessary. By replacing it with a different, physically motivated approach for describing how quantum systems combine, they identified a family of theories that can be expressed entirely with real numbers while remaining experimentally indistinguishable from conventional quantum mechanics.

Professor Bruß said: "This means that both frameworks yield identical predictions for any conceivable experiment. Within this framework, imaginary numbers are thus not fundamentally necessary in quantum mechanics and can in principle be replaced by alternative formulations using real numbers."
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough in quantum mechanics, showing that the theory can be formulated without imaginary numbers, which challenges a long-held assumption and could impact future quantum technologies. The findings have broad implications for physics and emerging quantum computing and communication technologies, providing a new foundational understanding with potential practical benefits.
articles.personality_title: Physicists show quantum mechanics can work without imaginary numbers
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Quantum mechanics is a science that explains how tiny particles like atoms behave. For many years, scientists thought this theory needed both real and imaginary numbers (special math tools) to describe what happens at the smallest scales.

**What happened** – Researchers from Heinrich Heine University Düsseldorf and the German Aerospace Center studied the math behind quantum mechanics. They found that the theory can be written using only real numbers, without imaginary numbers. Their work was published in the journal Physical Review Letters and highlighted by the American Physical Society.

**Impact** – This discovery challenges a long-held belief that imaginary numbers are essential in quantum mechanics. It means the theory might be simpler than we thought, and it could change how scientists develop new quantum technologies like quantum computers and secure communication.

**What's next step** – Scientists will explore how this new way of describing quantum mechanics might help improve technologies and deepen our understanding of the quantum world. More experiments will test if this real-number approach works in practical applications.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Quantum mechanics can be described using only real numbers, offering a new way to understand and develop quantum science and technology.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les physiciens montrent que la mécanique quantique peut fonctionner sans nombres imaginaires
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La mécanique quantique est une science qui explique comment se comportent les toutes petites particules comme les atomes. Pendant longtemps, les scientifiques ont pensé que cette théorie avait besoin à la fois de nombres réels et imaginaires (des outils mathématiques spéciaux) pour décrire ce qui se passe à ces petites échelles.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de l’Université Heinrich Heine de Düsseldorf et du Centre aérospatial allemand ont étudié les mathématiques derrière la mécanique quantique. Ils ont découvert que la théorie peut être écrite en utilisant seulement des nombres réels, sans nombres imaginaires. Leur travail a été publié dans la revue Physical Review Letters et mis en avant par la Société américaine de physique.

**Impact** – Cette découverte remet en question la croyance ancienne que les nombres imaginaires sont essentiels en mécanique quantique. Cela signifie que la théorie pourrait être plus simple que ce que l’on pensait, et cela pourrait changer la façon dont les scientifiques développent de nouvelles technologies quantiques comme les ordinateurs quantiques et les communications sécurisées.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques vont explorer comment cette nouvelle manière de décrire la mécanique quantique pourrait aider à améliorer les technologies et à mieux comprendre le monde quantique. D’autres expériences testeront si cette approche avec des nombres réels fonctionne dans des applications pratiques.

**Résumé en une phrase** – La mécanique quantique peut être décrite uniquement avec des nombres réels, offrant une nouvelle façon de comprendre et de développer la science et la technologie quantiques.
articles.personality_title_es: Los físicos demuestran que la mecánica cuántica puede funcionar sin números imaginarios
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La mecánica cuántica es una ciencia que explica cómo se comportan las partículas muy pequeñas como los átomos. Durante mucho tiempo, los científicos pensaron que esta teoría necesitaba tanto números reales como imaginarios (herramientas matemáticas especiales) para describir lo que ocurre a estas escalas tan pequeñas.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad Heinrich Heine de Düsseldorf y del Centro Aeroespacial Alemán estudiaron las matemáticas detrás de la mecánica cuántica. Descubrieron que la teoría puede escribirse usando solo números reales, sin números imaginarios. Su trabajo fue publicado en la revista Physical Review Letters y destacado por la Sociedad Americana de Física.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento cuestiona la creencia de larga data de que los números imaginarios son esenciales en la mecánica cuántica. Esto significa que la teoría podría ser más simple de lo que se pensaba, y podría cambiar la forma en que los científicos desarrollan nuevas tecnologías cuánticas como las computadoras cuánticas y la comunicación segura.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos explorarán cómo esta nueva forma de describir la mecánica cuántica podría ayudar a mejorar las tecnologías y profundizar nuestra comprensión del mundo cuántico. Se realizarán más experimentos para probar si este enfoque con números reales funciona en aplicaciones prácticas.

**Una frase clave** – La mecánica cuántica puede describirse usando solo números reales, ofreciendo una nueva forma de entender y desarrollar la ciencia y tecnología cuánticas.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of an elegant quantum wave pattern gently unfolding across a soft, natural-colored canvas, where one half of the wave is composed of flowing, intertwined real-number symbols represented as smooth, solid lines, and the other half subtly fades away, symbolizing the absence of imaginary numbers, all set against a serene abstract background evoking the harmony of fundamental physics without human figures or text.

articles.title: A 200-year-old physics experiment could help build future computers
articles.summary: Scientists at Nanyang Technological University in Singapore have discovered a surprisingly simple way to create exotic light structures called optical skyrmions using a 200-year-old optical effect known as the Poisson spot. Instead of relying on expensive, highly engineered materials, they simply shine a laser at a tiny circular disc, producing stable swirling patterns in light that researchers believe could one day help power advanced data storage, communications, and computing technologies.
articles.content:
Scientists at Nanyang Technological University, Singapore (NTU Singapore) have found a much simpler way to produce unusual light structures known as optical skyrmions by reviving a classic optics experiment that dates back more than 200 years.

Optical skyrmions are tiny, stable swirling patterns formed within the properties of light. Their structure has often been compared to the spines of a hedgehog. Because they can potentially encode and store information, researchers see them as promising building blocks for future data storage, communications, and computing technologies.

Instead of relying on expensive, highly engineered metamaterials that have traditionally been needed to generate optical skyrmions, the NTU team created them by shining a laser at a small circular disc. The approach provides a far simpler way to produce, study, and control these complex light structures.

The findings, published in the journal Optica, were led by Nanyang Assistant Professor Shen Yijie from NTU's School of Physical and Mathematical Sciences and School of Electrical and Electronic Engineering.

"What is remarkable is that optical skyrmions can now be generated using a simple effect where light bends around an object, without relying on expensive, complex man-made metamaterials or highly specialized techniques," explained Asst Prof Shen.

"This could make optical skyrmions much more accessible to researchers. By lowering the technical barrier to creating and studying them, the method opens up new possibilities for scientists to study how they could be used in future optical, materials and computing research."

A Classic Light Phenomenon Finds a New Purpose

The breakthrough is based on the Poisson spot, a well known optical phenomenon in which a bright point appears at the center of the shadow cast by a circular object when it is illuminated by a coherent light source such as a laser.

The Poisson spot played an important role in the early 19th century debate over the nature of light. At the time, scientists questioned whether light traveled only as particles in straight lines or behaved as waves that could bend and spread.

Wave theory predicted that a bright point should appear in the center of the disc's shadow, where complete darkness would otherwise be expected. Observing the Poisson spot provided convincing evidence that light undergoes diffraction, meaning it bends and spreads as it passes around objects or through small openings.

Four Types of Optical Skyrmions at Once

The researchers also discovered that their Poisson spot setup naturally produced as many as four related topological field patterns simultaneously.

These included spin skyrmions, Stokes skyrmions, electric field skyrmions, and magnetic field skyrmions. Spin refers to the rotation like properties of light, while the Stokes parameters describe polarization, or the direction in which light waves vibrate as they travel.

Generating these four types together could give scientists a unique opportunity to compare how different optical skyrmions form, evolve, and interact within the same light field.

Computer simulations showed the structures as swirling arrays of arrows that illustrate how different properties of light change direction across the Poisson spot.

A Simpler Way To Control Complex Light

Light possesses many characteristics that researchers can manipulate, including its intensity, phase, polarization, spin, and its electric and magnetic field vectors.

These properties can be arranged into topological structures, which are patterns that remain stable even when stretched or distorted. By adjusting the conditions that shape the light field, scientists may be able to precisely control the size, shape, and behavior of optical skyrmions.

Asst Prof Shen said: "In the light spot that we created, several types of optical vectors could form topological structures at the same time. These different components of light are closely connected, but they do not necessarily form identical topological patterns.

"Being able to produce and compare several skyrmions within one system could help researchers uncover new links between light's electric, magnetic and other physical properties."

Potential Applications in Computing and Photonics

Skyrmions were first proposed in particle and nuclear physics before later becoming an important area of study in condensed matter physics and magnetic materials. More recently, scientists have begun investigating optical skyrmions as stable, particle like structures that exist within light fields.

Earlier methods for producing optical skyrmions relied on metamaterials, which are artificially engineered microscopic structures designed to manipulate light in ways that conventional materials cannot.

By replacing those complex systems with a much simpler optical setup, the NTU team's work could make optical skyrmion research more accessible. The findings also provide a foundation for future studies of topological light and may contribute to advances in photonics, advanced materials, information processing, and next generation computing.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough in producing optical skyrmions using a simple, low-cost method based on a classic optical effect. This advancement lowers technical barriers and has broad implications for future computing, data storage, and communications technologies, representing a meaningful positive impact on technology development with detailed context.
articles.personality_title: Scientists revive 200-year-old light effect to create new patterns for future computers
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Optical skyrmions are tiny, stable swirling patterns in light that could help store and process information for future technologies. Until now, making these patterns required expensive and complex materials.

**What happened** – Researchers at Nanyang Technological University in Singapore found a simpler way to create optical skyrmions. They used a classic optical effect called the Poisson spot, which is a bright point of light that appears in the shadow of a small circular object when a laser shines on it. This method produces four types of optical skyrmions at once without needing costly materials.

**Impact** – This discovery makes it easier and cheaper for scientists to study and control optical skyrmions. Since these light patterns can store information, the new method could speed up research into advanced data storage, communications, and computing. It also helps scientists understand how different light properties interact.

**What's next step** – Researchers will use this simple setup to explore how to control and apply optical skyrmions in real devices. This could lead to new technologies that handle data faster and more efficiently using light.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A 200-year-old light effect is now a simple tool to create complex light patterns that may power future computers and communication technologies.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques réutilisent un phénomène lumineux vieux de 200 ans pour créer de nouvelles structures pour les ordinateurs du futur
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les skyrmions optiques sont de petits motifs lumineux stables qui pourraient aider à stocker et traiter des informations pour les technologies futures. Jusqu'à présent, leur fabrication nécessitait des matériaux coûteux et complexes.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des chercheurs de l'Université technologique de Nanyang à Singapour ont trouvé un moyen plus simple de créer ces skyrmions optiques. Ils ont utilisé un effet optique classique appelé le point de Poisson, un point lumineux qui apparaît dans l'ombre d'un petit objet circulaire lorsqu'un laser l'éclaire. Cette méthode produit quatre types de skyrmions en même temps sans matériaux coûteux.

**Impact** – Cette découverte facilite et réduit le coût des études sur les skyrmions optiques. Comme ces motifs lumineux peuvent stocker des informations, cette méthode pourrait accélérer la recherche sur le stockage de données, les communications et l'informatique avancée. Elle aide aussi à mieux comprendre comment les différentes propriétés de la lumière interagissent.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs utiliseront ce dispositif simple pour explorer comment contrôler et appliquer les skyrmions dans des appareils réels. Cela pourrait conduire à de nouvelles technologies pour traiter les données plus rapidement et efficacement grâce à la lumière.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Un effet lumineux vieux de 200 ans devient un outil simple pour créer des motifs lumineux complexes qui pourraient alimenter les ordinateurs et communications du futur.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos reutilizan un efecto de luz de 200 años para crear nuevos patrones para computadoras futuras
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los skyrmions ópticos son pequeños patrones de luz estables que podrían ayudar a almacenar y procesar información para tecnologías futuras. Hasta ahora, hacer estos patrones requería materiales caros y complejos.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad Tecnológica de Nanyang en Singapur encontraron una forma más simple de crear skyrmions ópticos. Usaron un efecto óptico clásico llamado punto de Poisson, que es un punto brillante de luz que aparece en la sombra de un objeto circular pequeño cuando se ilumina con un láser. Este método produce cuatro tipos de skyrmions ópticos a la vez sin necesidad de materiales costosos.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento facilita y abarata el estudio y control de los skyrmions ópticos. Como estos patrones de luz pueden almacenar información, el nuevo método podría acelerar la investigación en almacenamiento avanzado, comunicaciones y computación. También ayuda a entender cómo interactúan diferentes propiedades de la luz.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores usarán este sencillo método para explorar cómo controlar y aplicar los skyrmions ópticos en dispositivos reales. Esto podría llevar a nuevas tecnologías que manejen datos más rápido y eficientemente usando luz.

**Resumen en una frase** – Un efecto de luz de 200 años es ahora una herramienta simple para crear patrones de luz complejos que podrían impulsar las computadoras y tecnologías de comunicación del futuro.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a glowing laser beam illuminating a small circular disc, casting a soft shadow with a bright, swirling central light spot composed of intricate, colorful spiraling patterns resembling hedgehog spines made of intertwined arrows and waves, symbolizing the complex, stable optical skyrmions and their dynamic light properties in a simple, elegant scientific setup.

articles.title: Scientists discovered the brain doesn't make decisions the way we thought
articles.summary: A new study suggests the brain begins making decisions much earlier than scientists previously thought. Researchers found that even primary sensory regions are influenced by higher brain areas through rapid feedback loops, rather than simply passing information forward. This more dynamic view of brain function could help engineers design future AI systems that think more like biological brains while using far less power.
articles.content:
Scientists at the University of Illinois Urbana Champaign have uncovered evidence that could reshape how researchers think about both the brain and artificial intelligence. Their findings suggest that decision making begins much earlier in the brain than traditional theories propose, offering fresh ideas for designing future AI systems that are more capable and far more energy efficient.

Led by electrical and computer engineering professor Yurii Vlasov at The Grainger College of Engineering, the research was published in Proceedings of the National Academy of Science (PNAS). The study points to an unexpected role for early sensory brain regions in decision making, challenging the long accepted view that decisions emerge only after information moves through a strict hierarchy of brain regions.

Rethinking How the Brain Makes Decisions

The human brain is widely regarded as the most complex structure in the known universe. Scientists still do not fully understand how it works, which is why reverse engineering the brain was identified by the National Academy of Engineering in 2008 as one of the 14 grand challenges for engineering in the 21st century.

For decades, many artificial intelligence systems, including convolutional neural networks, have been inspired by the idea that the brain processes information in a one way sequence. According to this traditional model, sensory information travels upward through increasingly complex brain regions until it reaches the frontal cortex, where decisions are made.

Vlasov and other researchers have increasingly questioned whether that picture is complete.

Instead, they are exploring a model based on natural intelligence, which has been refined through evolution over hundreds of millions of years. In this framework, the brain does not rely only on a step by step flow of information. Decision making also depends on interconnected feedback loops that allow information to move in both directions between brain regions.

Because biological intelligence performs remarkably complex tasks while using far less energy than today's AI systems, understanding this architecture could help guide the development of future artificial intelligence.

"We want to learn from a billion years of evolution," Vlasov said. "How is that biological intelligence organized architecturally? Can we learn from the architectural side of the brain and emulate that to make AI more effective, less power hungry, and more intelligent than it currently is? In the level of decision-making, that's where current AI is lacking."

Early Brain Regions Show Decision Making Activity

To investigate how these processes work, the research team focused on the brain's earliest stages of sensing and perception.

The scientists recorded neural activity in mice as they navigated a virtual reality corridor and made perceptual decisions. They found evidence of decision related activity in the primary somatosensory cortex (S1), one of the brain's earliest sensory processing areas.

Rather than simply passing information forward, S1 appeared to be influenced by higher brain regions through feedback loops. This top down regulation suggests that decision making involves continuous communication across multiple brain areas instead of a simple one direction flow of information.

"The neural code of the brain is still mostly an unknown language," Vlasov said. "But this systems-level understanding can be viewed as a potential impact on how more efficient artificial neural networks can be built -- how the next generation of AI can be thought through. Maybe with these analogies that we learn from real brains, we can improve AI further."

What the Findings Could Mean for Future AI

The researchers emphasize that the study does not provide a blueprint for building better artificial intelligence. Instead, it offers new insights into how the brain organizes decision making that could eventually inspire future AI architectures.

Next, Vlasov and his team plan to investigate the timing of these brain signals in greater detail. They also intend to develop new technologies for measuring neural activity to better understand how feedback loops emerge and coordinate different levels of brain processing.

"By looking at the fast temporal dynamics of neural activity, maybe we can understand better how these feedback loops are engaged in making decisions," Vlasov said. "Maybe that's the approach that potentially uncovers these currently unknown mechanisms -- how these feedback loops are organized dynamically and how they form and shape different levels of processing. Maybe that can be implemented in new architectures for AI."
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery about brain decision-making processes that challenges traditional models and offers promising insights for developing more efficient and intelligent AI systems. This breakthrough has broad implications for both neuroscience and AI technology, potentially benefiting society by enabling energy-efficient AI inspired by biological intelligence. The article provides sufficient detail on the research, its context, and future directions, making it a substantive and inspiring advancement.
articles.personality_title: New study shows brain starts decisions earlier than we thought
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have long believed that the brain makes decisions only after sensory information moves step-by-step through different brain areas, ending in the front part of the brain where choices are made.

**What happened** – Researchers at the University of Illinois found that decision making actually begins much earlier, in the brain’s primary sensory regions. They studied mice navigating a virtual corridor and saw that these early brain areas are influenced by signals from higher brain regions through fast feedback loops, not just by passing information forward.

**Impact** – This discovery changes how we understand brain function, showing that decision making involves continuous two-way communication between brain parts. It also offers new ideas to build artificial intelligence (AI) systems that think more like real brains but use much less energy.

**What's next step** – The scientists will study the timing of these brain signals more closely and develop better tools to observe how these feedback loops work. Their goal is to learn more about brain communication to inspire smarter and more efficient AI designs.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Brain decisions start earlier than thought, involving feedback between brain regions, a finding that could help create better and more energy-efficient AI.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une nouvelle étude montre que le cerveau commence à décider plus tôt que prévu
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques pensaient que le cerveau prenait des décisions seulement après que l’information sensorielle ait traversé plusieurs zones du cerveau, jusqu’à la partie frontale où les choix sont faits.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de l’Université de l’Illinois ont découvert que la prise de décision commence en fait beaucoup plus tôt, dans les premières zones sensorielles du cerveau. Ils ont étudié des souris dans un corridor virtuel et ont vu que ces zones sont influencées par des signaux venant des zones supérieures du cerveau via des boucles de rétroaction rapides, pas seulement en transmettant l’information vers l’avant.

**Impact** – Cette découverte change notre compréhension du fonctionnement du cerveau, montrant que la prise de décision implique une communication continue dans les deux sens entre les parties du cerveau. Elle donne aussi de nouvelles idées pour construire des systèmes d’intelligence artificielle (IA) qui pensent comme le cerveau réel mais consomment beaucoup moins d’énergie.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques vont étudier plus précisément le timing de ces signaux cérébraux et développer de meilleurs outils pour observer ces boucles de rétroaction. Leur but est de mieux comprendre la communication cérébrale pour inspirer des IA plus intelligentes et efficaces.

**Phrase clé** – Les décisions du cerveau commencent plus tôt que prévu, grâce à des échanges entre zones cérébrales, une découverte qui pourrait aider à créer des IA meilleures et moins énergivores.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevo estudio muestra que el cerebro empieza a decidir antes de lo pensado
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos creían que el cerebro tomaba decisiones solo después de que la información sensorial pasaba por varias áreas cerebrales, hasta llegar a la parte frontal donde se toman las decisiones.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad de Illinois descubrieron que la toma de decisiones comienza mucho antes, en las primeras áreas sensoriales del cerebro. Estudiaron ratones en un corredor virtual y vieron que estas áreas son influenciadas por señales de regiones cerebrales superiores a través de bucles de retroalimentación rápidos, no solo transmitiendo la información hacia adelante.

**Impacto** – Este hallazgo cambia cómo entendemos el funcionamiento del cerebro, mostrando que la toma de decisiones implica comunicación continua en ambas direcciones entre partes del cerebro. También ofrece nuevas ideas para crear sistemas de inteligencia artificial (IA) que piensen más como cerebros reales y usen mucha menos energía.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos estudiarán con más detalle el tiempo de estas señales cerebrales y desarrollarán mejores herramientas para observar cómo funcionan estos bucles de retroalimentación. Su objetivo es entender mejor la comunicación cerebral para inspirar diseños de IA más inteligentes y eficientes.

**Frase clave** – Las decisiones cerebrales comienzan antes de lo pensado, con intercambios entre regiones cerebrales, un hallazgo que podría ayudar a crear IA mejores y con menos consumo de energía.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of an abstract neural network shaped like interconnected glowing pathways and loops, with early sensory brain regions symbolized as softly illuminated nodes at the base, dynamically linked by flowing feedback loops to higher, elevated nodes; the scene evokes a harmonious, evolving system of communication and decision-making inspired by biological intelligence, rendered in natural, muted colors.

articles.title: Maya mathematician’s name decoded alongside astronomical formula
articles.summary: Hieroglyphs on the wall of a Maya building record calculations concerning the orbits of Earth, Mars and Venus, as well as the name of a mathematician who wrote the text around 1200 years ago
articles.content:
Hieroglyphs on the wall of a Maya building record calculations concerning the orbits of Earth, Mars and Venus, as well as the name of a mathematician who wrote the text around 1200 years ago

The Maya solar calendar is made up of 19 months zimmytws/Getty Images

An ancient Maya astronomer-mathematician has been identified for the first time along with his complex calculations made around 1200 years ago, predicting the orbital cycles of Mars and Venus.

“This is the first direct mention of an ancestral Maya astronomer-mathematician by personal name,” says Franco Rossi at the Massachusetts Institute of Technology.

It is also the oldest recorded name of an astronomer-mathematician ever known from anywhere in the Americas, he says.

Advertisement

The Maya civilisation flourished in Central America between roughly 2000 BC and AD 1697. They had advanced knowledge of mathematics and astronomy, but much of it was lost after the mass burning of their books by Spanish missionaries.

Since 2010, excavations at the site of Xultun, Guatemala, have revealed astronomical and mathematical inscriptions inside a small masonry building.

Subscriber-only newsletter Sign up to Our Human Story Sign up to newsletter

On the east and north-east wall of the building are around 50 texts that scientists believe are “rough drafts” made by Maya mathematicians as they charted and predicted the cycles of celestial objects relative to Earth and to one another.

Rossi and his colleagues have painstakingly deciphered one of these murals, named Text 19. At the bottom of the mural is the name of Sak Tahn Waax, which translates to White-chested Fox, who is believed the be the author of the formula.

The mathematical formula inscribed on a wall at the Maya site of Xultun, Guatemala F.D. Rossi; H. Hurst

Text 19 consists of 11 hieroglyphs, which had to be scanned, photographed and magnified under different illumination angles, and compared to other, later, astronomical-mathematical writings, before their meaning could be deduced.

While similar mathematical and astronomical expertise is found across Maya cities, the mention of Sak Tahn Waax, who the researchers believe was probably male, is unique.

“Whether this is an instance of the scribe himself signing his own calculation or attributing the intellectual work to another, we have a formula and the name of its creator, which serves to demonstrate the importance of this kind of intellectual contribution for Classic Maya people,” says Rossi.

The calendar system on display in Text 19 uses maths in relation to time periods, he says. These time periods were drawn from a 260-day calendar, a 365-day solar calendar, a 584-day approximation of Venus’s synodic cycle (when the planet returns to the same position relative to both Earth and the sun) and a 780-day approximation of Mars’s synodic cycle. The total length of the formula is five Venus synodic cycles or 2920 days, and the date that Text 19 most likely refers to is 7 November of AD 781 in the Julian calendar.

Exactly how this formula would have been applied is unknown, says Rossi, as it “isn’t incorporated into any larger body of work”.

“We think it is meant to concisely and meaningfully show the relationship between these two planets and human counts of time in ways that could then be applied to political ceremony, predictive astronomy and understandings of seasonality,” he says.

Such meticulous mathematical legwork would have been critical to structuring life in a world before computers, smartphones and weather apps, says Rossi.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant archaeological and scientific discovery: the identification of an ancient Maya astronomer-mathematician and his complex astronomical calculations from 1200 years ago. This breakthrough provides meaningful insight into early advanced mathematical and astronomical knowledge with broad cultural and historical significance, inspiring curiosity and appreciation for human intellectual achievement.
articles.personality_title: Ancient Maya mathematician identified with detailed planetary calculations
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – The Maya civilization, which lived in Central America over a thousand years ago, was known for its advanced knowledge of math and astronomy. However, much of their written work was lost after Spanish missionaries destroyed many of their books.

**What happened** – Archaeologists working in Xultun, Guatemala, discovered hieroglyphs on the wall of a small building. These writings include complex calculations about the orbits of Earth, Mars, and Venus. For the first time, they also found the name of the mathematician who created these formulas, Sak Tahn Waax, meaning White-chested Fox. This discovery dates back about 1200 years.

**Impact** – This is the oldest known name of an astronomer-mathematician in the Americas. It shows that Maya scholars made detailed predictions about planetary movements using their calendars. The formulas combine different time cycles, like the 260-day and 365-day calendars, and the 584-day and 780-day cycles of Venus and Mars, respectively. These calculations helped the Maya plan ceremonies and understand seasons long before modern tools existed.

**What's next step** – Researchers will continue studying the other inscriptions in the building to learn more about Maya science and how these formulas were used in daily life. This could reveal more about how ancient people observed and predicted natural events.

**One-sentence takeaway** – For the first time, scientists have named an ancient Maya mathematician and uncovered his complex work predicting the movements of planets more than a thousand years ago.
articles.personality_title_fr: Un mathématicien maya ancien identifié avec des calculs planétaires détaillés
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La civilisation maya, qui vivait en Amérique centrale il y a plus de mille ans, était connue pour ses connaissances avancées en mathématiques et en astronomie. Cependant, une grande partie de leurs écrits a été perdue après la destruction de nombreux livres par des missionnaires espagnols.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des archéologues travaillant à Xultun, au Guatemala, ont découvert des hiéroglyphes sur le mur d’un petit bâtiment. Ces écrits contiennent des calculs complexes sur les orbites de la Terre, de Mars et de Vénus. Pour la première fois, ils ont aussi trouvé le nom du mathématicien qui a créé ces formules, Sak Tahn Waax, qui signifie « Renard à la poitrine blanche ». Cette découverte remonte à environ 1200 ans.

**Impact** – C’est le plus ancien nom connu d’un astronome-mathématicien en Amérique. Cela montre que les savants mayas faisaient des prédictions précises sur les mouvements planétaires en utilisant leurs calendriers. Les formules combinent différents cycles de temps, comme les calendriers de 260 et 365 jours, ainsi que les cycles de 584 et 780 jours de Vénus et Mars. Ces calculs aidaient les Mayas à organiser des cérémonies et à comprendre les saisons bien avant les outils modernes.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs continueront d’étudier les autres inscriptions dans le bâtiment pour en apprendre davantage sur la science maya et comment ces formules étaient utilisées dans la vie quotidienne. Cela pourrait révéler comment les anciens observaient et prédisaient les événements naturels.

**En résumé** – Pour la première fois, des scientifiques ont nommé un mathématicien maya ancien et découvert son travail complexe prédisant les mouvements des planètes il y a plus de mille ans.
articles.personality_title_es: Matemático maya antiguo identificado con cálculos planetarios detallados
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La civilización maya, que vivió en América Central hace más de mil años, era conocida por su avanzado conocimiento en matemáticas y astronomía. Sin embargo, gran parte de sus escritos se perdió después de que los misioneros españoles quemaran muchos de sus libros.

**Qué pasó** – Arqueólogos en Xultun, Guatemala, descubrieron jeroglíficos en la pared de un pequeño edificio. Estos escritos incluyen cálculos complejos sobre las órbitas de la Tierra, Marte y Venus. Por primera vez, también encontraron el nombre del matemático que creó estas fórmulas, Sak Tahn Waax, que significa Zorro de pecho blanco. Este hallazgo tiene unos 1200 años.

**Impacto** – Este es el nombre más antiguo conocido de un astrónomo-matemático en las Américas. Muestra que los mayas hacían predicciones detalladas sobre el movimiento de los planetas usando sus calendarios. Las fórmulas combinan diferentes ciclos de tiempo, como los calendarios de 260 y 365 días, y los ciclos de 584 y 780 días de Venus y Marte. Estos cálculos ayudaban a los mayas a planear ceremonias y entender las estaciones mucho antes de que existieran las herramientas modernas.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores seguirán estudiando las otras inscripciones en el edificio para aprender más sobre la ciencia maya y cómo se usaban estas fórmulas en la vida diaria. Esto podría mostrar cómo los antiguos observaban y predecían eventos naturales.

**Resumen en una frase** – Por primera vez, los científicos han identificado a un matemático maya antiguo y descubierto su trabajo complejo para predecir el movimiento de los planetas hace más de mil años.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of an ancient Maya stone wall inside a small masonry building, adorned with intricate hieroglyphic carvings symbolizing planetary orbits of Earth, Mars, and Venus, alongside a stylized white fox silhouette representing the mathematician Sak Tahn Waax, all rendered in natural earthy tones and soft light to evoke reverence for ancient astronomical knowledge.
